  <meta name="description" content="8 mental health rules I swear by- to use whether you’re in therapy or not:

1. No screens while you eat.
Most people don’t know this, but the state of mind we’re in while we eat impacts how we digest our food. Eating can be a natural 15-minute mindfulness break if we give ourselves the permission to use it that way.

2. If it takes less than 2 minutes, do it now.
Or similarly, the “one touch rule.” Micro-avoidances stack up and quietly drain our mental bandwidth. If it’s under 2 minutes, knock it out and watch your mental load clear up. For the one-touch rule: if you touch a task, complete it. You end up saving oodles of time.

3. Name the emotion before trying to understand it. 
Our minds love stories, especially when we’re activated. Labeling the feeling first (anxious, disappointed, jealous, lonely) lessens its power over us and makes us much more capable of understanding its origin, purpose, and message.

4. Use grounding in the first moments of your day.
When I wake up, the first thing I do is say a prayer thanking the Creator for being alive, and then I put my feet on the floor and FEEL THEM THERE, sending a clear signal to my body and mind that I am grounded, present, and supported. It deeply impacts how I feel the rest of the day.

5. Do a gut check before saying “yes.”
When I’m not sure which path is right, I ask my body. And I’ve spent years honing this practice. When we practice this enough, we learn to interpret the signals our bodies are sending, and we stop letting logic talk us into things our bodies are rejecting.

6. No work or problem-solving after 9 pm.
Nighttime thinking is rarely insightful. It’s usually just fatigue mixed with nervous urgency. If worry thoughts show up, write them down on paper and tell yourself, “Tomorrow I’ll be better equipped to handle this very important thing.” Protect your evening hours like they’re the most important part of your workday. They are.

7. Use both/and dialectical mindset.
You can be grateful AND struggling at the same time
time. You can love yourself fully AND want to change in the same breath. We gaslight ourselves too often by saying we should be just one thing or the other, when in reality, we will always be both. You are allowed to honor the complexity of any experience. In fact, you should.

8. When you’re spiraling, go to the body before the brain. A dysregulated body cannot be reasoned with. Drink water, eat a protein-rich snack, step outside for 3 minutes, or do 10 slow breaths. Then, and only then, decide how you’re going to tend to your inner world.

Therapy In The City, PLLC

#therapistsoflinkedin #moderntherapy  #therapeuticrelationship 
#clinicalwisdom #mentalhealthprofessionals  #realtalktherapy 
#TherapyThatHeals #HealingJourney #EmotionalHealing  | 11 comments on LinkedIn">

  <div class="attributed-text-segment-list__container relative mt-1 mb-1.5 babybear:mt-0 babybear:mb-0.5">
    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
        " dir="ltr" data-test-id="main-feed-activity-card__commentary">8 mental health rules I swear by- to use whether you’re in therapy or not:

1. No screens while you eat.
Most people don’t know this, but the state of mind we’re in while we eat impacts how we digest our food. Eating can be a natural 15-minute mindfulness break if we give ourselves the permission to use it that way.

2. If it takes less than 2 minutes, do it now.
Or similarly, the “one touch rule.” Micro-avoidances stack up and quietly drain our mental bandwidth. If it’s under 2 minutes, knock it out and watch your mental load clear up. For the one-touch rule: if you touch a task, complete it. You end up saving oodles of time.

3. Name the emotion before trying to understand it. 
Our minds love stories, especially when we’re activated. Labeling the feeling first (anxious, disappointed, jealous, lonely) lessens its power over us and makes us much more capable of understanding its origin, purpose, and message.

4. Use grounding in the first moments of your day.
When I wake up, the first thing I do is say a prayer thanking the Creator for being alive, and then I put my feet on the floor and FEEL THEM THERE, sending a clear signal to my body and mind that I am grounded, present, and supported. It deeply impacts how I feel the rest of the day.

5. Do a gut check before saying “yes.”
When I’m not sure which path is right, I ask my body. And I’ve spent years honing this practice. When we practice this enough, we learn to interpret the signals our bodies are sending, and we stop letting logic talk us into things our bodies are rejecting.

6. No work or problem-solving after 9 pm.
Nighttime thinking is rarely insightful. It’s usually just fatigue mixed with nervous urgency. If worry thoughts show up, write them down on paper and tell yourself, “Tomorrow I’ll be better equipped to handle this very important thing.” Protect your evening hours like they’re the most important part of your workday. They are.

7. Use both/and dialectical mindset.
You can be grateful AND struggling at the same time
time. You can love yourself fully AND want to change in the same breath. We gaslight ourselves too often by saying we should be just one thing or the other, when in reality, we will always be both. You are allowed to honor the complexity of any experience. In fact, you should.

8. When you’re spiraling, go to the body before the brain. A dysregulated body cannot be reasoned with. Drink water, eat a protein-rich snack, step outside for 3 minutes, or do 10 slow breaths. Then, and only then, decide how you’re going to tend to your inner world.

  <div class="attributed-text-segment-list__container relative mt-1 mb-1.5 babybear:mt-0 babybear:mb-0.5">
    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
        attributed-text-segment-list__content-truncated" dir="ltr" data-test-id="main-feed-activity-card__commentary">Shocking Reality: Are You Truly Prioritizing Your Mental Health 🧠

You've tried mindfulness apps and "positive thinking," but you still feel overwhelmed. Here is the question, Are You Even Truly Prioritizing Your Mental Health? 💤 

The decreasing stigma around this topic has opened up a crucial conversation, but it's time to move beyond awareness and into action. 

As a counselor and healer, I see the silent struggles beneath the surface of our hyper-connected, high-pressure world every single day. 

We are expected to be always-on, productive, and resilient, yet we are rarely taught the fundamental skills to manage the emotional fallout of that expectation.

We're sold a narrative of success that includes juggling a demanding career, a vibrant social life, and personal well-being. 😲 

Often, burnout is an emotional and energetic depletion caused by feeling like your efforts aren't making a difference, or by the constant negotiation of your own boundaries.

Think of it less as an empty tank and more as a frayed rope. The issue isn't a lack of effort; it's the invisible toll of unaddressed emotional debt. 😫

One of the most common issues I encounter is the belief that setting boundaries is a form of rejection. The truth is, setting a boundary is an act of self-preservation and a clear signal of self-respect. It’s about defining the space where you can show up as your best self.

Another crucial but often misunderstood aspect of mental health is the power of emotional intelligence. 🔥

We tend to think of it as "being in tune with our feelings," but it's more strategic than that. 

A key component of emotional intelligence is understanding that your feelings are data, not destiny. 

Feeling anxious doesn't mean you are a permanently anxious person; it means there is an underlying issue—a tight deadline, a difficult conversation, or a fear of the unknown—that needs to be addressed. 💡🎯 

By reframing emotions as information, we move from being victims of our feelings to becoming active problem-solvers. This is the core of overcoming limiting beliefs. 

The belief "I'm not good enough" isn't a fact; it's a feeling, a signal that we need to examine the source of that insecurity and challenge the narrative.

The modern world, particularly the hybrid work model, has blurred the lines between our personal and professional lives. 

Navigating burnout in this environment requires establishing new "digital boundaries." This isn't just about turning off notifications; it's about creating mental and emotional separation fiercely. Reclaim your holistic health. 📵

Try it. You'll be surprised at how much energized you feel. 😌
Accept and start the REAL WORK now. ❤️

💎Like, follow for more.
😇 DM me to explore.
 
💎 Your "Self-Centered Rituals" are actually enriching your energy. 🚀
 
🔁 Share with someone you know. Let's create a life we love! 💫✨

  <div class="attributed-text-segment-list__container relative mt-1 mb-1.5 babybear:mt-0 babybear:mb-0.5">
    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
        " dir="ltr" data-test-id="main-feed-activity-card__commentary">Not Every Mental Health App Is Helping You.

We have seen the most rapid growth in the mental health area. A field unknown to laymen for decades and over the last few years, mental health apps have exploded. Meditation apps, mood trackers, CBT bots, journaling platforms, therapy-matching services. They are marketed as affordable, accessible, stigma-free solutions to emotional distress. And for many people, they are the first point of contact with mental health support. That matters. But it also deserves serious scrutiny.

Most people download these apps during vulnerable moments. Late nights. Breakdowns. Burnout. Loneliness. Anxiety spirals. The decision is emotional, not rational. “Maybe this will help.” Obviously, while going through distress, it’s difficult to be rational, as in reading a certain app’s reviews, how valid they are, and how it was founded. When you get a ray of hope in distress, which these apps offer you, take your first chance to grab it. Companies know this.

These apps do well in terms of marketing and how it should be as well, for real. Using calming colours, soft voices, reassuring language, and promises of improvement. “Feel better in 10 minutes.” “Reduce anxiety fast.” “Scientifically proven.” - (They activate placebo effects and expectation bias.) This seems good for the moment, but again, it’s very temporary in nature. 

Many apps are based on simplified versions of therapeutic techniques. Breathing exercises, gratitude lists, basic CBT worksheets, and mindfulness recordings. These tools are not harmful by themselves. In therapy, they are useful when guided, personalised, and contextualised. But in apps, they are generic. They assume everyone needs the same intervention. Mental health does not work that way.

A person with trauma, depression, burnout, or neurodivergence will not benefit equally from the same exercises. But apps rarely assess this deeply. They prioritise scalability over suitability. One size fits all is easier to sell. 
For mild stress, this might be fine. For deeper distress, it can delay real help.
Someone struggling with chronic anxiety might think that the app is not faulty, it’s them who’s “not doing it properly.” This is dangerous. 

There is also the risk of emotional outsourcing. When people rely heavily on apps, they may stop seeking human support. Instead of talking to a friend, they log a mood. Instead of seeking therapy, they subscribe to another app. The app becomes a substitute, not a supplement.

Access is important. But access without quality is not care. Convenience without responsibility is not support. Scaling without safeguarding is not progress. 

I am going to another post, part 2, because I really have MORE to add onto this.
</p>

  <div class="attributed-text-segment-list__container relative mt-1 mb-1.5 babybear:mt-0 babybear:mb-0.5">
    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
        " dir="ltr" data-test-id="main-feed-activity-card__commentary">Hello connection....👋🏻
                I Am glad to share my article about "MENTAL HEALTH"....💗
          

The Modern Landscape of Mental Well-being
Mental health is more than the absence of illness; it is the emotional, psychological, and social fuel that dictates how we handle stress, relate to others, and make choices. Current trends highlight a growing integration of AI-driven support and neurodiversity awareness, signaling a future where mental health care is personalized rather than "one size fits all."
However, with rising rates of burnout and digital fatigue, the challenge remains: how do we stay stable when the world feels like it's moving at 100 mph?
 
5 Strategies for Lasting Mental Stability
To keep your mental "battery" charged and your mind resilient, consider these evidence-based habits:

1. Master "Digital Hygiene"
Our brains aren't designed for 24/7 information loops.
 * The Action: Implement a "Cognitive Cooldown." Set a non-negotiable time (e.g., 9:00 PM) to silence notifications.
 * The Benefit: This reduces cortisol levels and prevents "doom-scrolling" from hijacking your sleep cycle.

2. Prioritize "Micro-Boundaries"
You don't need to quit your job to find peace; you need to protect your energy in small ways.
 * The Action: Practice saying "no" to one optional commitment a week.
 * The Benefit: Preventing emotional fatigue starts with recognizing that your time is a finite resource.

3. Move for the Mind, Not Just the Body
Physical activity is one of the most effective "antidepressants" available.
 * The Action: Aim for the "Sweet Spot"—about 45 minutes of movement (walking, yoga, or dancing) 3–5 times a week.
 * The Benefit: Exercise releases endorphins and helps regulate the autonomic nervous system, making you less reactive to daily stressors.

4. Foster Intentional Connection
Isolation is a silent driver of anxiety.
 * The Action: Use the "3-Touch Rule." Every week, reach out to one friend, check in on a family member, and engage with a community group (even a hobby-based online forum counts).
 * The Benefit: Consistent social contact builds a psychological safety net.

5. Embrace "Analog Play"
In a world of screens, our brains crave tactile, unproductive fun.
 * The Action: Spend 15 minutes on a hobby that has nothing to do with your career—drawing, gardening, or even building Legos.
 * The Benefit: "Play" reduces stress and increases optimism by allowing the mind to focus on the present moment without the pressure of a "result."
> A Quick Reminder: > Mental health is a journey, not a destination. It's okay to have "low-battery" days; the goal is to have the tools ready to help you recharge.

    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
        " dir="ltr" data-test-id="main-feed-activity-card__commentary">Mental health is not a luxury; it's a necessity for sustainable success. 💼🌱

Today's fast-paced work environment often leads to burnout. Understanding the triggers of stress and learning how to manage them can significantly improve our professional and personal lives.

I’ve put together an in-depth resource on Mental Health and Stress Management to help professionals navigate these challenges effectively.

Read the full article here: <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https%3A%2F%2Flnkd%2Ein%2FgHRAHuDa&urlhash=4iVW&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" rel="nofollow" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">https://lnkd.in/gHRAHuDa</a>

  <div class="attributed-text-segment-list__container relative mt-1 mb-1.5 babybear:mt-0 babybear:mb-0.5">
    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
        " dir="ltr" data-test-id="main-feed-activity-card__commentary">🧠 Mental Health Monday | Week 13

Emotional Regulation in High-Intensity Jobs

In first responder roles, emotional regulation isn’t about not feeling. It’s about surviving moments that demand control, speed, and composure. Over time, the nervous system adapts to intensity in ways that can be beneficial in the short term but costly in the long term.

Anger vs. Stress Response
Irritability is often misunderstood as “anger.” Clinically, it’s more commonly a sign of nervous system overload. When sleep debt, cumulative stress, and repeated exposure stack up, the brain shifts into a low-threshold threat response. Small stressors feel big because the system is already maxed out.

Emotional Numbing
When emotions feel muted or absent, it’s not because you don’t care. It’s because your nervous system has learned to dampen sensation to stay functional. Numbing is a protective response, not a personality change. The risk is staying there too long.

On-Shift Emotional Containment (“Boxing It”)
Temporarily compartmentalizing emotions during a call is often necessary and adaptive. The key distinction is that containment is intentional and time-limited. Suppression is unintentional and ongoing. One protects performance, the other builds pressure.

Releasing It Later — Why Suppression Backfires
When emotions are never discharged, they surface sideways, such as irritability, shutdown, hypervigilance, or sudden overwhelm. The nervous system doesn’t forget what the mind avoids. Safe release (movement, breath work, structured processing, therapy) allows the body to complete the stress cycle.

Grounding for Flashbacks (On Scene or Off Duty)
Grounding isn’t about calming down. It’s about orienting to now. Naming what you see, feel, and hear in real time helps the brain differentiate past threat from present safety, reducing reactivity without shutting you down.

Why You Feel Fine… Until You’re Not
High performers often function well for long stretches, then hit a wall. This isn’t a weakness. It’s delayed nervous system fatigue. Stress responses don’t always show up immediately; they surface when the system finally has space to feel.

For many veterans, the body remains in “combat mode” long after service ends. This is a learned survival state, not a failure to adjust. With the right support, the nervous system can relearn safety without losing strength, discipline, or identity.

If any of this resonates, support doesn’t mean something is “wrong.” It means your nervous system has been doing exactly what it was trained to do and may need help standing down.

If this shows up for you. You’re not broken. Your nervous system has been working hard to keep you functional under pressure. Support can help you regulate without losing control, performance, or identity. If you’d like to talk with one of our providers, please call our office at 847-550-4520 to schedule an appointment, either in person or via telehealth.</p>

  <div class="attributed-text-segment-list__container relative mt-1 mb-1.5 babybear:mt-0 babybear:mb-0.5">
    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
        " dir="ltr" data-test-id="main-feed-activity-card__commentary">Each person has different needs and <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fenergy&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">#energy</a> levels. Some people thrive when constantly moving, while others need time to decompress. Your needs for sleep, <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fself&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">#self</a>-care, <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fnutrition&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">#nutrition</a>, exercise and work-life balance will differ from others
. You will know you have found the proper flow when you feel at peace with yourself with the amount of busyness versus rest and work versus play.  

Creating a support network
People with high-functioning anxiety may believe they must deal with their behaviors alone because they fear <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fcriticism&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">#criticism</a> or negative outcomes. A positive support network of people who care for you, regardless of outcomes, can help ease anxiety symptoms.
Identifying core values
Some people with high-functioning anxiety become fixated on society-defined <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fsuccess&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">#success</a>, like having the "right" job, car, house and material possessions. These items often are important only because other people believe they are important. A <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Ftherapist&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">#therapist</a> can help you uncover what is important to you outside society's expectations, and align your thoughts and actions with your core values.

<a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fpracticing&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">#Practicing</a> <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fmindfulness&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">#mindfulness</a>
This is the practice of purposefully being aware of and focusing on the present moment. Concentrating on one thing or moment can increase feelings of calm and peace.
Establishing healthy boundaries

This can help improve <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Frelationships&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">#relationships</a> with others and establish rules for yourself. The importance of saying "no" often is talked about as part of boundary setting. That's because many people overextend themselves. But some people with high-functioning anxiety also should embrace saying "yes" to opportunities that stretch their comfort levels. Their lives grow in experiences and fulfillment the more they face their fears.

Learning to accept criticism
This can be difficult for many people but especially people with high-functioning anxiety. They may snap back or get <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fdefensive&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">#defensive</a>. A therapist can help you identify ways to step back at this moment and evaluate the feedback neutrally without emotion.

While <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fhigh&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">#high</a>-functioning anxiety may not be a recognized diagnosis, it represents a significant subset of people who experience anxiety symptoms while maintaining a high level of functionality. These people face internal struggles like persistent self-doubt, fear of failure, and a constant drive for perfection and pleasing others, which profoundly affect their daily lives.

Talk with your health care team if you are experiencing anxiety symptoms like persistent self-doubt, fear of failure and a constant drive for perfection. They can connect you to therapy and help you develop coping strategies so you can foster healthier ways of managing your anxiety and lead a fulfilling life.

- <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fworry&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">#Worry</a>
- <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Ffear&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">#Fear</a>
- <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Foverthinking&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">#Overthinking</a>
- <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fnervousness&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">#Nervousness</a>
- <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fpanic&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">#Panic</a>

  <div class="attributed-text-segment-list__container relative mt-1 mb-1.5 babybear:mt-0 babybear:mb-0.5">
    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
        " dir="ltr" data-test-id="main-feed-activity-card__commentary">The 2026 Mental Health Zeitgeist

Mental health moves in waves. What we prioritize, what we question, and what we leave behind inevitably shapes our conversations and our nervous systems. In 2026, we are clearly moving away from fixing, optimizing, and labeling, and toward regulation, environmental context, and nervous-system safety.
And honestly, I am here for it.
Below are some of the key terms shaping the 2026 mental health zeitgeist and why they matter.

1) Nervous System Regulation

AKA: The official replacement for generic “self-care.”
Language we’re seeing includes nervous system hygiene, regulation over coping, emotion dysregulation, co-regulation, and a renewed focus on the window of tolerance.
This trend reflects collective burnout from mindset-only advice. People are realizing we cannot out-think chronic stress, trauma responses, or nervous system overload. Regulation-first approaches acknowledge the body, not just the mind.

2) Functional Freeze

AKA: Also known as high-functioning shutdown.
This looks like showing up to work, staying productive, and maintaining routines while internally feeling numb, stuck, or emotionally paralyzed.
This term resonated because it finally named survival-mode productivity and challenged the myth that functioning equals wellness. It reduced shame around a question many are asking: How can I be doing well on paper and still not feel okay?

3) Emotional Burnout (Beyond Work)
AKA: Burnout is no longer just about jobs.
We’re hearing more about emotional burnout, compassion fatigue outside of healthcare, decision fatigue, and life-admin exhaustion.
As autonomy at work increased post-pandemic, many realized burnout stems from overwhelming life demands, prolonged unhealthy relationships, constant decision-making, and excessive personal responsibility. Life saturation, not just work stress, is the real culprit.

4) Attachment Awareness Goes Mainstream

AKA: Attachment theory is no longer niche.
Terms like anxious-avoidant loops, earned security, protest behaviors, and trigger versus wound are now common language.
The shift in 2026 is powerful: less fixation on labels and more focus on choice, accountability, and how we show up in relationships. This movement away from accommodation and toward intentional relating is a meaningful evolution.

5) Therapy Jargon Backlash
AKA: Popularity does not equal accuracy.
Phrases like “that’s a boundary,” “you’re gaslighting me,” “protect your peace,” and “do the work” are being critically re-examined.
People are recognizing that therapy language, when oversimplified or weaponized, can avoid accountability and shut down repair. The pushback reflects growing discernment and a demand for context, nuance, and real responsibility.

  <div class="attributed-text-segment-list__container relative mt-1 mb-1.5 babybear:mt-0 babybear:mb-0.5">
    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
        " dir="ltr" data-test-id="main-feed-activity-card__commentary">Sleep has always been a cornerstone of productivity—but lately, mental health challenges have made it harder to rest well. I’m learning to approach it holistically:
	•	Keeping a consistent sleep and wake schedule
	•	Creating a calm, dark, and cool sleep environment
	•	Practicing mindfulness, journaling, and gentle routines before bed
	•	Prioritizing mental health support when needed
	•	Balancing daytime habits like sunlight exposure, movement, and limited caffeine

Small changes are helping me restore better sleep and mental clarity. For anyone else navigating similar challenges, what strategies have worked for you? Sharing experiences can normalize the conversation about mental health and self-care in the workplace.”*

Hashtags: <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fmentalhealthawareness&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">#MentalHealthAwareness</a> <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fsleepwell&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">#SleepWell</a> <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fworklifebalance&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">#WorkLifeBalance</a> <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fselfcare&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">#SelfCare</a></p>
